topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp parchment felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the official notification. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that stole the air from my lungs. The words detailing the full academic grant, the prestigious university acceptance—they were all there, meticulously printed, yet they suddenly warped into something grotesque. I reread the section about the designated recipient, my eyes darting back and forth across the page, searching for an error, a typo that could explain this impossible discrepancy. Nothing. The name listed was not mine. A cold, creeping sensation began to coil in my stomach, tightening until I felt nauseous. I dropped the paper onto the desk, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet dorm room. My hands started to tremble uncontrollably, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to scrub the text from my vision, but the image of that other person’s future, secured by my own name, refused to leave.

[story 2]

I stared at the acceptance letter, the ink blurring into meaningless shapes. Every line screamed my name, my GPA, my desperate hopes, yet the designated scholarship recipient’s name sat starkly in bold typeface. A slow, dreadful realization washed over me, starting as a dull throb behind my temples and escalating into a blinding wave of panic. I stood up so abruptly my chair scraped backward across the floor. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, trapped bird. I snatched the letter, my fingers slick with sudden sweat, and scanned the fine print again, my vision tunneling. The reality of the situation settled over me like a physical weight, crushing my chest. I couldn't breathe properly; the air felt thick and unyielding. I started pacing the small room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, muttering incoherent denials to the empty space, desperately trying to reconcile the tangible document in my hand with the dizzying impossibility of what I was seeing.

[story 3]

When the envelope was finally opened, the sheer scale of the deception hit me like a physical blow. I sat frozen on the edge of my bed, the paper trembling in my grasp. The meticulous formatting, the official seals—it was all a carefully constructed lie, and I was the unwitting centerpiece of the fraud. A strange numbness began to spread through my limbs, making it difficult to feel anything at all. I looked down at my hands; they seemed alien, clumsy instruments capable only of holding a terrible secret. A low, strangled sound escaped my throat, a sound more animal than human. I couldn't move. My gaze was fixed on the printed name, and a wave of profound disorientation swept over me, leaving me stranded in a silent, terrifying void where certainty used to reside. I slowly lowered the paper, my shoulders slumping as if the weight of the discovery had physically collapsed my frame.
